prior_learning: Use questioning to see what students already know and can work with mathematically. Only teach pictograms, for example, if students still have misconceptions around them.
common_misconceptions: Students don’t use the key in a pictogram and because of that misinterpret information.
Students cannot remember the differences between the mean, median and mode.  They need to know about the limitations of each type of average.
Students sometimes believe the range is a measure of central tendency when it’s a measure of spread.
Students need to understand the differences between discrete and continuous data and which graphs are appropriate for each.

Students struggle to hold a pair of compasses properly, holding both arms so that circles are not drawn accurately. They need to be able to draw accurate circles with a pair of compasses before attempting bisectors or triangle constructions.

learning_outcomes: Interpret scatter graphs.
Understand what is meant by an outlier.
key_knowledge_components: I know what is meant by an outlier and can identify one on a scatter diagram.
I can draw and interpret scatter graphs.
I know when it would be appropriate to draw a scatter diagram.

key_vocab: Scatter diagram, variable, correlation, positive correlation, negative correlation, outlier, trend, pattern

key_questions: Ask students questions about a given scatter diagram to help them read and interpret them eg
If I scored 20 marks in my maths what would you expect me to get for art?
When would  a scatter diagram be an appropriate graph to draw? (When there is a relationship between 2 variables)
Which point is an outlier (see graph below)

What would you expect the correlation to be like between the temperature outside and ice cream sales? What about the price of sweets and sweet sales? What about the size of someone’s feet and how good they are at maths?
teaching.suggested_activities_summary: Start by showing a scatter diagram and asking questions to help students read and interpret it (see key questions for examples). Once they increase in confidence get students to think about when a scatter diagram would be an appropriate graph to use – get them to look at the patterns in the points, for example, how some scatter diagrams form an upwards line (positive correlation) and some a downwards line (negative correlation). The closer the p…

learning_outcomes: Mean, median and mode
key_knowledge_components: I know how to calculate measures of central tendency.
I can find the range, mode, median and mean of a set of data, and use them to compare data sets.

key_vocab: average, mean, median, mode, modal class, frequency, total, outlier

key_questions: How do I work out an average?
What averages are there?
How do I calculate the median, the mode, the mean?
What are the differences between each average? Which average is the best in this situation?
Why is the mode not a useful average for this data?
(Sometimes there is more than one mode or no mode)
teaching.suggested_activities_summary: Start by questioning students to see what they already know. Students will have learned about averages before but often struggle to remember the differences between the averages so give them a knowledge organiser for this (example under ‘addressing gaps’). Give them questions on modes in a variety of contexts eg, list of numbers, bar chart: Make sure they understand that there can sometimes be more than 1 mode and sometimes there may not be a mo…

notes.addressing_gaps_summary: SEND learners have a Pupil Passport that informs strategies for learning bespoke to that student. Staff must make sure they are familiar with the various strategies involved and embed these in their lessons. Students often struggle with the differences in how to calculate each type of average. Give them a method for remembering this eg

learning_outcomes: Best averages for different data sets; bias
Range
key_knowledge_components: I know what a range is.
I can find the range, mode, median and mean of a set of data, and use them to compare data sets.

key_vocab: average, mean, median, mode, modal class, frequency, total, outlier

key_questions: How do I calculate the range?
What does the range tell me if it is a large number?
What about if it is a smaller number?
If you all sat a test and your scores had a range of 2 marks what would that tell me about the class? What about if your scores had a range of 30 marks?
teaching.suggested_activities_summary: Re-cap mean, median and mode from previous lesson. Introduce the range and give students some questions where they calculate the range. Spend time ensuring students understand that the range is a measure of spread/variation and it is not another average. Talk to students about different contexts when one type of average is better than others. Explain how an outlier can distort the mean and give examples of this: If the range is a small number th…

notes.addressing_gaps_summary: SEND learners have a Pupil Passport that informs strategies for learning bespoke to that student. Staff must make sure they are familiar with the various strategies involved and embed these in their lessons. Students often confuse range as one of the types of averages. It needs to be explicitly taught so that they understand that it is a measure of spread/variation.

learning_outcomes: Comparing 2 data sets in terms of average and consistency
key_knowledge_components: I can compare data sets using measures of central tendency and a measure of spread.
I can provide appropriate reasons about the best measure of central tendency to use based on the context of the data.

key_vocab: average, mean, median, mode, modal class, frequency, total, outlier

key_questions: Which average would you use for this question? Why?
Why would the mean not be a good average to use this time?
Why would the mode not be a useful average to use for this data set? ( 45, 32, 57, 47, 68)
teaching.suggested_activities_summary: Start with a quiz or other that revises how to calculate the mean, median and mode and range. Then model an example where you compare 2 data sets eg Give a modelled example where you calculate the mean, the range and then write a couple of sentences stating which set had the higher average and which set had the smaller range and was therefore, more consistent. Give some questions for students to practise

learning_outcomes: Drawing and interpreting grouped and ungrouped frequency tables
key_knowledge_components: I can complete and interpret frequency tables.

key_vocab: Data, tally chart, frequency, frequency table, bar chart, quantitative data, qualitative data

key_questions: How could we collate this data as we are doing the experiment?
How could we group the tallies so that they’re easier to count?
What do we call the table when we add the frequency column?
What do we mean by quantitative data? What about qualitative? Give me an example to explain.
Look at this set of data – we cannot do a tally for each of these values as there’s so many so we need to group them.  What would sensible group intervals be (class intervals)?
teaching.suggested_activities_summary: Students will have covered tally charts and bar charts before so this lessons is a revision lesson to manage any student misconceptions. Start by doing a data collection experiment and use this to get students to understand the difference between a tally chart and a frequency table, why we group tally marks in 5s as well as the importance of giving a bar cart a title, labelling the axes, having an evenly spaced scale and having the frequency axi…

notes.addressing_gaps_summary: SEND learners have a Pupil Passport that informs strategies for learning bespoke to that student. Staff must make sure they are familiar with the various strategies involved and embed these in their lessons. Where students struggle to choose appropriate class intervals give them examples where this bit is done for them: Once they increase in confidence take away this support.

learning_outcomes: Mean from a frequency table
key_knowledge_components: I can find averages and the range from frequency tables, included ungrouped tables.

key_questions: How can I find a quicker way to find the mean of a large set of data?
What steps do I need to make? Can you tell me the steps in order?
teaching.suggested_activities_summary: Give an example where finding the mean would take a lengthy period of time because of the size of the data set. Put the data into a frequency table to group it. See below for example: Demonstrate how you can add in an extra column where each piece of data is multiplied by its frequency; these are then added up and divided by the sum of the frequencies to calculate the mean. Model an example including an example where the frequency table is set u…

notes.addressing_gaps_summary: SEND learners have a Pupil Passport that informs strategies for learning bespoke to that student. Staff must make sure they are familiar with the various strategies involved and embed these in their lessons. Some students will need templates with the additional column added on to remind them that each piece of data needs to be multiplied by its frequency.

learning_outcomes: Median and mode from a frequency table
key_knowledge_components: I can find averages and the range from frequency tables, included ungrouped tables.

key_vocab: Mean, frequency table, mode, median, estimate, class interval, data set, discrete data, continuous data

key_questions: How would I find the mode when the data is presented in a frequency table? What is the definition of the mode?
Can you find the exact mode or can you only estimate it?
How would I find the median when the data is presented in a frequency table?
Can I find the exact median when the ‘heights’ are given as a class interval?
What’s the modal number of siblings?
What’s the median number of siblings?
What’s the modal number of minutes late? Can you give an exact number or do you need to give an estimate (class interval)?
What’s the median number of minutes
teaching.suggested_activities_summary: Re-cap mean, median and mode when the data is given as a list. Re-cap the process for finding the mean when the data set is given in a frequency table. This lesson is about modes and medians when the data set is presented as a frequency table.  You should look at data for both discrete data sets and grouped or continuous data sets as in the example below: Start with discrete data and then continuous or grouped discrete data. See examples under k…

learning_outcomes: Estimating averages from a grouped frequency table
key_knowledge_components: I can find averages and the range from frequency tables, included ungrouped tables.
I can provide appropriate reasons about the best measure of central tendency to use based on the context of the data.

key_vocab: Mean, frequency table, mode, median, estimate, class interval, data set, discrete data, continuous data, midpoint

key_questions: How would I find the mean from a frequency table when the data set has grouped data?
Can I find the exact mean or will it be an estimate?
In this class interval what is the midpoint of each class?
What’s an easy way to find a midpoint? (Add biggest and smallest numbers in the class interval and then divide by 2)
teaching.suggested_activities_summary: Students will need a Steps 2 Success for this: Start by posing questions that get students to predict how they could find the mean from a frequency table when the data is grouped.  They need to understand that it will be an estimate only.  They also need to know how to find midpoints of each class interval. See questions under ‘key questions’. Model an example for the class eg., Then give students a couple to try themselves.

notes.addressing_gaps_summary: SEND learners have a Pupil Passport that informs strategies for learning bespoke to that student. Staff must make sure they are familiar with the various strategies involved and embed these in their lessons. Students need to know how to read and interpret class intervals. See Addressing Gaps in previous lesson. Students need to be confident with finding midpoints. Check they can do this before modelling an example. Students may need the addition…

learning_outcomes: Drawing and interpreting pictograms
key_knowledge_components: I can draw and interpret bar chats, dual bar charts and pictograms.

key_vocab: Data, tally chart, frequency,  frequency table, bar chart, quantitative data, qualitative data, dual bar charts, pictogram

key_questions: What is a pictogram?
Why would you choose to draw a pictogram rather than a bar chart?
teaching.suggested_activities_summary: A pictogram is just a different, more visual, bar graph.  Students need to take note of the key on each pictogram as this determines the frequencies. Give students practice at using the key to calculate frequencies: Once they are confident at reading and interpreting give them data sets to draw pictograms to display the information. They need to choose their key carefully.

notes.addressing_gaps_summary: SEND learners have a Pupil Passport that informs strategies for learning bespoke to that student. Staff must make sure they are familiar with the various strategies involved and embed these in their lessons. Some students will struggle to use the key to interpret frequencies.  Spend time near the beginning of the lesson giving them practice at this. See examples under suggested teaching activities.

key_questions: What common mistakes do people make when they are drawing a bar chart?
When would we use a dual bar chart?
teaching.suggested_activities_summary: We normally use a dual (or multiple ) bar chart when we want to compare 2 or more sets eg This lesson should be used to both read and interpret dual bar charts as well as drawing them for given data sets. Include examples where the bars are set up horizontally: When students are competent at reading and interpreting dual bar charts give them some data sets where a dual bar chart would be useful to visually compare the data sets eg Give students …

Students may need help with deciding the labels of each bar, spacing the bars out evenly and remembering to label axes, give the graph a title etc.

learning_outcomes: Discrete and continuous data
Drawing and interpreting histograms
key_knowledge_components: I can draw and interpret bar chats, dual bar charts and pictograms.

key_vocab: Discrete data, continuous data, bar chart, histogram

key_questions: What’s the difference between discrete data and continuous data?
Give me an example of some data that is continuous? (height, weight, hand span).
Give me an example of data that is discrete?
What differences can you see between a bar chart and a histogram?
teaching.suggested_activities_summary: Clarify with student the difference between discrete data and continuous data. For discrete data they should draw a bar chart whereas for continuous data they should draw a histogram. They need to note that the labels on the horizontal axis are in the under the middle of each bar for a bar chart whereas they are on the edge for a histogram. See example below.  Notice also for a histogram the bars are joined up. Model an example of drawing a hist…

notes.addressing_gaps_summary: SEND learners have a Pupil Passport that informs strategies for learning bespoke to that student. Staff must make sure they are familiar with the various strategies involved and embed these in their lessons. Some students will struggle with labelling the horizontal axis and may need this scaffolding done for them initially.

How do I hold the compass so that its arms don’t move when I am using it?
How do I use the protractor to measure an angle?

Students need to be confident with this before moving onto constructing triangles. So if they are nor confident spend the first lesson teaching this (see year 8 CTD HT3).
